data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Spikes
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 1.1 percentage points to 31.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the fourth consecutive week.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 9.7 percentage points to 21.7%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 86th time in 88 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 10.9 percentage points to 46.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the fifth consecutive week.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 12.0 percentage points to –14.4%. The bull-bear spread is unusually low and is below its historical average of 6.5% for the fifth consecutive week.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what impact the military conflict with Iran is having on their short-term outlook for stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • It is bearish for stocks: 38.0%.
  • It is a reason to be a little more cautious: 33.5%.
  • It creates a buying opportunity: 21.9%.
  • No impact: 4.6%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.1%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 31.9%, down 1.1 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 21.7%, down 9.7 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 46.4%, up 10.9 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Altimmune: The High Cost Of Going Solo (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Altimmune, Inc. (ALT) is a curious stock. By all metrics, it’s undervalued/underappreciated. Its GLP-1/glucagon agonist, pemvidutide, is prepping for a Phase 3 MASH program. Even conservative probability of success/peak share estimates result in a risk-adjusted NPV well over its paltry ~$200M enterprise value. So what’s going on?

My last write-up on ALT was in January and followed receipt of Breakthrough Therapy Designation for pemvidutide in MASH. The company had also just detailed 48-week Phase 2 data, in which pemvidutide improved non-invasive markers of fibrosis and liver health. The drug was also well-tolerated (especially relative to its class), and high-dose patients lost 7.5% of their weight.

However, MASH is becoming increasingly competitive, and Altimmune may be a few years behind its competitors. And a pivotal MASH program is no small venture without a partner. Altimmune’s Phase 3 plan also raised eyebrows. “No titration” (unlike other GLP-1s like WEGOVY) used to be a differentiating feature for pemvidutide, but management wants to up the dose in Phase 3 (to 2.4 mg), and to get there will require titration. This will, simultaneously, impact pemvidutide’s tolerability, which was its other differentiating feature (higher doses usually result in more side effects).

ALT is down 27.2% since my “cautious Hold” rating, and I saw the company has provided further details on its MASH aspirations (see Q4 call transcript, press release).

So I took another look below.

MASH

Pemvidutide’s Phase 3 MASH program design has been finalized. It will be a single global pivotal trial specifically structured to support an accelerated approval filing.

The “dual-cohort” approach is interesting. To me, it shows that while the FDA is interested in non-invasive tests for MASH, biopsy remains the “goldstandard.” In fact, the biopsy cohort will serve as the basis for the accelerated approval filing at 52 weeks. So the other cohort is there mainly to provide the safety database/long-term clinical outcome data required for full approval.

An 1,800-patient trial is a massive undertaking for a company with just ~$340M in cash. But management signaled that it is preparing to go it alone, arguably at the expense of shareholders.

We've also remained focused on strengthening our financial position. The $75 million capital raise completed in January was an important step in our ongoing efforts to prepare for the planned initiation of our Phase III this year. We remain committed to securing access to the capital required to successfully drive our clinical programs and create long-term value.

The decision to include the 2.4 mg dose is likely an attempt to increase weight loss. MASH and obesity often go hand-in-hand, so prescribers may prioritize the most potent weight loss agent. Management appears to be positioning pemvidutide as a salvage therapy for patients who cannot tolerate the GI-associated side effects and complex titration of market leaders like Novo Nordisk's (NVO) WEGOVY and Eli Lilly's (LLY) ZEPBOUND (pending Phase 3 outcomes and approval). In this context, Altimmune doesn’t appear interested in going face-to-face with its fiercest competitors. They are trying to establish a niche in MASH. This seems wise, and even a niche within MASH can still = a blockbuster opportunity. Still, the elephant in the room is that “everyone and their moms” are also trying to secure niches in MASH.

It’s actually quite insane and should leave investors wondering, “Where does this leave pemvidutide?”

So, when modeling pemvidutide in MASH, we have to be realistic, right? There are millions of MASH patients, but treatments like REZDIFFRA (MDGL), WEGOVY, and ZEPBOUND (pending approval) will likely be prioritized.

Then you have big pharmas like Roche (RHHBY) and Boehringer Ingelheim—two of the largest drug companies in the world—vying for a piece of the MASH pie as well.

So my peak share estimate leans conservative at 2.5%. But even 2.5% implies 125,000 patients taking pemvidutide. The big kicker is annual pre-launch R&D costs. Phase 3 MASH programs can cost anywhere between $300M and $500M when accounting for liver biopsies, central pathology labs, manufacturing scale-up, and long-term follow-up costs.

Anyways, I’m not going to go over every detail because I don’t want anyone falling asleep. I have pemvidutide achieving $1.5B in peak annual sales, which I think would be a major victory for Altimmune. Its risk-adjusted NPV is $638.1M.

Pipeline

Outside of MASH, Altimmune is also evaluating pemvidutide in AUL and AUD.

AUD (NCT06987513) is technically a psychiatric condition, and the odds of a Phase 2 psych-directed drug coming to market are low. The AUD trial’s primary endpoint is "change from baseline in the average number of heavy drinking days per week using the TLFB method," which is usually self-reported and, therefore, subject to “noise” because patients often reduce drinking just by being in a clinical trial. Obviously, that is the reason for enrolling to begin with. And AUD usually features more than just a “physical craving,” like anxiety, depression, or trauma. Then, you have to consider that there are established therapies in AUD that have been used for decades. Are prescribers and payers going to jump on a “liver-directed” branded drug just because it's "on-label?"

ALD patients (NCT07009860) are usually much sicker than MASH patients, and they are at a higher risk for sudden liver failure. Then you have to deal with developing a long-term treatment for a population that is struggling with alcohol addiction. So dropout rates may be a lot higher. And if the patients are still drinking, is pemvidutide suddenly going to be “protective?”

Also bear in mind that both indications will cost a lot in Phase 3, given the chronic and large populations.

So, when I modeled these indications, both ALD and AUD came back at a negative risk-adjusted NPV (see Appendix for both Pharma DCF cards).

This may strike you as “shocking,” but bear in mind that most clinical assets never make a penny. So, having a “negative” risk-adjusted NPV is not as uncommon as it seems.

What’s actually most shocking is my modeled funding gap. Advancing a MASH, AUD, and ALD candidate through late-stage trials is going to be very expensive. To make up for the modeled $800M funding gap, I had to account for 22% annual dilution, which raises ~230M new shares at $3.5/share (a slight discount to ALT’s current price). This implies that the dilution we are seeing of late is just the beginning.

So I think this is the reason why the market is leaning skeptical, and it’s hard to blame them.

MASH alone would be enough weight to carry around. Add on low-probability/high-cost bets in AUD and ALD, and you have a funding nightmare.

Financial Health

Altimmune ended FY25 with $274M in cash and investments. $75M was added in January following a registered direct offering (bringing the pro forma total to $340M per management). Management believes they have enough cash to fund operations "into 2028," which is a year before pemvidutide's modeled launch in MASH.

In the trailing twelve months, Altimmune's "cash burn" (cash from operations) was $67.5M. While management's estimate appears consistent with its historical cash burn, investors should anticipate an increase in cash burn as the company advances pemvidutide into costly late-stage programs and preps for a pemvidutide launch in MASH.

Given that pemvidutide is still a few years away from accelerated approval and several years away from hard clinical outcomes that payers will pay up for (e.g., mortality, liver failure), without some sort of partnership, Altimmune will likely have to resort to dilution/debt to raise more money. I suspect that, even after accelerated approval in MASH, Altimmune will need at least a couple more years before becoming "self-sustaining."

Conclusion

ALT is a prime example of how a stock can appear undervalued, but for good reasons. At a glance, “pemvidutide in MASH, AUD, and ALD,” even with conservative inputs, should (and do) get you over Altimmune’s EV, but, as is usual, things are more complicated.

Funding each of these pursuits is a massive undertaking for a company the size of Altimmune…and I haven’t even addressed (when it comes to “funding gap”) the costs associated with launching said products in competitive markets.

So I’m inclined to “stay on the sidelines” until there is a better funding solution than dilution (rating downgrade to “Sell”).

Some things that could change my mind are overwhelmingly positive Phase 2 AUD results later this year that increase both POS and peak share estimates, a partnership with a large pharma to help fund R&D expenditures, and a strategic pivot away from low rNPV pursuits like ALD and AUD.

Appendix

data.title: Fastenal: Poised For Another Decade Of Clockwork Results
data.description:
I began covering Fastenal Company (FAST) in 2021. What I first thought of as being just another boring industrial supply company ended up showing up as a highly disciplined, innovative, and technology-driven supply chain juggernaut.

Since it’s my first article about Fastenal and I know not many are familiar with the name, it's only fair that I present what the company does. I could just summarize it with their motto: “great people, close to your business, empowered by technology”, but I’ll briefly explain it.

Fastenal is the largest fastener supplier in North America. They sell a broad range of industrial and construction supplies (e.g., threaded fasteners, bolts, screws, wire rope, metal framing systems, cutting tools, safety products etc.) through a global network of locations utilizing diverse technologies such as vending devices, bin stock devices, and eBusiness (e-commerce sales, mostly to traditional customer base as supplement to other channels). The large majority of their transactions are business-to-business.

First conceived in the early 2000s, the vending machines used to store fasteners, MRO items, tools, and multiple other products, through what is called Fastenal Managed Inventory (FMI), are the backbone of the company’s ability to outperform its peers.

Fastenal founder Bob Kierlin's genius was recognizing that something as boring as a bolt, sold with extreme reliability and convenience at an industrial scale, could build an extraordinary business. Instead of a factory worker stopping production to go find a bolt, the right fastener is always stocked right on the factory floor and automatically reordered when inventory runs low.

I view FAST as a bullish long-term investment, one of the finest industrial compounders in the market, driven by its market share gains, digital transformation, and potential for accelerated growth in 2026. However, at current prices, the valuation leaves little margin of safety. I rate Fastenal a Hold - this is not a verdict on the business, but on the price. Below, I will detail why.

What History Tells Us

The table below illustrates what brilliant execution looks like. Consistent, regular growth in revenue, gross, and net income margins. All that while barely having any net debt and maintaining strong free cash flow growth.

The last 15 years for Fastenal have been stellar. The company maintains a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of +8,1% for both revenue and EBITDA, with net margin growing from 13% to 15%. It also grew EPS from $0.30 in 2011 to $1.10 in 2025 - nearly 4x in 14 years, with no single year of negative EPS growth. This level of consistency impresses and is rarer than the CAGR alone conveys.

FAST has been able to maintain healthy growth and is still growing dividends year after year. The business pays close to 100% of all Free Cash Flow (FCF) in dividends — after it already paid for operations, CapEx, and interest. In fact, by returning nearly everything it earns to shareholders, it prevents equity from compounding faster than earnings, and the company can structurally maintain a high ROE.

ROE grew from 25% to 32% since 2011, through genuine margin expansion (FMI acceleration, onsite-scaling). That, coupled with being a Dividend Aristocrat, translated to a consistent 19% annual share price returns to shareholders since its IPO in 1987, and a whopping +212.984,45% total return, when adjusted for dividends reinvested.

That’s an impressive compound for a company that, for most, is off the radar. It’s not a sexy business like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), or Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), and it doesn’t need to be. Industrials are not sexy. Fastenal is one of those companies you can add to your portfolio, and it’ll probably never upset you or give you a negative surprise.

As Benjamin Graham once famously said:

In the short run, the market is a voting machine. In the long run, it's a weighing machine.

As the chart illustrates, short-term divergences between price and earnings are inevitable — but over decades, the correlation is undeniable. Today's divergence, with price running slightly ahead of earnings growth, is precisely what my fair value estimate reflects.

Near-term market volatility can create attractive entry points for long-term investors - and given current valuations, patience may well be rewarded. Many studies show that, in the long term, stock prices are strongly positively correlated with companies' profits, and this has been proven right with Fastenal, as shown in the chart above. It’s a business that runs like clockwork.

Current Market Tailwinds

In the Q4 report, Fastenal cited persistently sluggish market conditions, with U.S. PMI and industrial production mixed, which translated to manufacturing segments' relative weakness. PMI averaged 48.3 last quarter, not dramatically low but still below 50.

The latest February reading on the ISM Manufacturing PMI has told us things are slowly getting back on track, at 52.4, following January's 52.6 reading. This is the second straight reading above 50, which usually indicates an expansion in the manufacturing sector, after a long contraction period, as seen in the chart above.

This is especially important for Fastenal since around 30% of sales come from fasteners, and product categories more closely aligned with final goods production (OEM represents 20% of sales) tend to be more significantly impacted by periods of weak industrial production.

This is one of the reasons the management has been quietly shifting new contracts towards Maintenance, Repair and Operations (MRO), to reduce its cyclical exposure, and has recently reported stronger growth in MRO-oriented lines such as electrical, plumbing, and janitorial, as well as becoming the Official Supply Chain Partner of the NHL, and also signing individual club deals.

Ultimately, the continuation of this PMI expansion should certainly pose as a powerful tailwind for Fastenal, especially considering its fastener expansion project, which I'll comment on in more detail later.

CEO Change And What It Means For Operations

Former CEO Dan Florness is stepping out this year as both Head of the company and as a board member, remaining until 2028 only as a strategic adviser to newly appointed CEO Jeff Watts. Both men have been with the company for nearly 30 years. Watts is currently the President of Fastenal and has been the Chief Sales Officer (CSO) since 2023.

In 2023, Fastenal's board deliberated on the creation of the CSO chair in an attempt to reorganize and optimize its commercial structure. Jeff Watts was selected for the position and became responsible for the company's commercial strategy refresh, setting priorities, goals, and supervising global sales activities.

Since then, the focus has totally shifted towards accelerating larger customer acquisition. This is where, by far, they get most of their revenue already.

The number of $50,000+ sites (a subset of $10,000+ customer sites) increased 14% year-over-year and accounts for more than half of total sales. At the same time, <$5,000 sites decreased by 12%. With 8.000 less sites overall, the business generated $200 million more in sales.

This strategy is combined with a shift from a branch-oriented service to focus on onsite locations growth (already 54% of all inventory is staged locally or within customer sites), extensive use of FMI technology, and eBusiness integration. In Q4 2025, 46.1% of sales were through FMI technology, versus 43.9% in Q4 2024, and the total installed base grew to more than 136,000 units.

The signing goal is again increased to between 28,000 and 30,000 units in 2026. Sales through its total digital footprint accounted for 61.4% of total sales in Q4 2025, and this year's goal is to reach 66%. This supports a more stable revenue stream as it increases switching costs for customers and improves customer retention, while it increases Fastenal's margins and drives its operating leverage.

Another important topic is FAST's fastener expansion project. It's a company-wide effort coordinated across sales, supply chains, and operations, to ensure easy fulfillment and distribution of key SKUs—mainly, MRO-fasteners. This is significant because MRO-fasteners are Fastenal's highest-margin core product, and the business literally started as a fastener shop.

In the Q4 report, Dan Florness commented that the fastener expansion project was what eventually helped offset the gross margin headwinds from onsite location expansions (that require dedicated headcount before revenue kicks in) and new FMI installations (upfront costs). High-margin tailwind offsets low-margin headwind.

Fastenal is already reaping results from this new commercial effort, with fastener sales notably growing over 15% in September, outpacing total company sales growth on that month. It also led to outperformance in manufacturing end markets and outpaced growth in non-fastener categories.

In the end, it's a matter of operational efficiency improvement, market share gains, and margin expansion. Fastenal only had 5 CEOs in nearly 60 years (average tenure of over a decade per leader). I believe that the CEO transition will be smooth and Fastenal will maintain its current focus on growing sites and sales with its large customers, and continue digitalization of its service for the next decade.

Potential Risks

The ongoing US-Iran conflict is now in its second week, but for the most part, I don't consider near-term geopolitical events to present real risks to most stocks, especially industrials. In fact, since World War II, the S&P 500 has seen a median gain of 0,4% two weeks after the geopolitical event and an 11.2% gain 12 months after it.

However, the problem with the US-Iran War is about the Strait of Hormuz and how long it will stay shut down. Fastenal sources fasteners extensively from China and Taiwan, and a possible escalation could make freight costs higher and create inventory availability risk. The duration of this disruption remains uncertain, but I expect any impact on Fastenal to be modest and temporary, as it has pricing power.

Oil prices could pose a threat if higher for longer, leading to a possible industrial demand contraction and pushing PMI below 50 again. Although Fastenal is heading in the right direction, the growing MRO revenue base is relatively defensive in this scenario.

What I do consider is whether the US economy is actually capable of delivering its promise of redeveloping the country's domestic industry, following last year's tariff announcements. Early signs are encouraging - Apple recently committed to investing $500 billion in US manufacturing over four years - but the next few Manufacturing PMI readings will be crucial to the stock's near-term performance, as it's a good thermometer of the sector's health.

Additionally, the recent CEO change has everything to be a seamless transition, given Mr. Watts' long tenure with the company and his current key role as sales culture developer and operations-guided leader. We should expect Fastenal to keep delivering strong results.

Valuation

Fastenal became a member of the select group of 69 Dividend Aristocrat companies in 2024, after delivering 27 years of consecutive dividend growth. The Gordon Dividend Growth model is therefore the natural framework to value FAST, since it has been paying out close to 100% of its annual Free Cash Flow for the past two decades.

Current full-year dividends are projected at $0.96 per share for 2026. Assuming payout stays roughly the same, I've considered a dividend growth of 12% for the next 7 years, in line with its 3, 5, and 10-year dividend CAGR, with a required return of 8%.

A terminal growth rate of 4%, slightly below the historical long-term nominal US GDP growth rate of 5%, completes the model. The result is an intrinsic value of $39.99 per share, implying a downside of 14.17% from today's price of $46.59 to its fair value.

The Gordon Model is highly sensitive to the spread between the discount rate and terminal growth rate, as the sensitivity table above illustrates. I consider 8% and 4% to be reasonable but conservative assumptions for a business of Fastenal's quality and track record.

The model's credibility is supported by recent price history. The stock ended last year quoted at $40.13, practically at my fair value estimation, and now is trading for $46.59, up 16.10%, reflecting strong Q4 numbers and recent PMI readings.

Applying Fastenal's 5-year average P/E of 33.01x to a projected 2026 EPS of $1.22—derived from its 10-year EPS CAGR of 11%—implies a fair value of $40.30, remarkably close to my Gordon Model estimate of $39.99. While I consider mean reversion to the 5-year average unlikely given Fastenal's structural improvements in recurring revenue, the convergence of two independent methods below market price significantly reinforces the Hold rating.

Conclusion

Great businesses like Fastenal are rarely cheap. They command a premium for their "safety", operational excellence, culture, governance, and solid overall fundamentals. My estimation of a fair value should be used as a guide for a good entry point, rather than a verdict on whether it is a deserving business to own in a portfolio or not.

My current rating for Fastenal is a Hold—the stock is running ahead of my fair value estimate, but the business itself continues to execute flawlessly.

I will be watching the next PMI readings, FMI signings growth, and the first quarters under new CEO Jeff Watts closely, and will not hesitate to upgrade if the price becomes right. The company has proven its ability to deliver for decades, and I expect it to continue doing so through the next one.

data.title: MSOS: The Question Is Not If, But 'When' The Rescheduling Will Occur
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS). This article is the continuation of my initial coverage thesis published on December 17, 2025, also with a Buy recommendation. My intention with this article is to revisit this thesis, investigate what happened, and evaluate the risk-return relationship again.

Context

In my career writing articles for Seeking Alpha, I've been able to help many investors make better investment decisions through my articles on stocks and my weekly series on macroeconomics and hard assets.

However, my recommendation to buy ETFs that track the cannabis industry amid expectations of marijuana reclassification was not assertive. Mistakes and successes are part of the daily life of an analyst and investor. As Ray Dalio would say, mistakes are more important for learning than successes, so let's study.

AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF

MSOS is actively managed by AdvisorShares, its expense ratio is 0.78%, and the ETF has $778 million in AUM. I chose to write about MSOS because it was the first ETF exclusively dedicated to tracking the cannabis industry. Another important detail is that the manager uses swaps since a large part of these companies' shares are traded on the over-the-counter market. The main holdings are below:

What caused the collapse in December?

Basically, the executive order came milder than the market expected. It was not a "reclassification," but rather an instruction to the Attorney General to complete the reclassification in the most expeditious manner permitted by law. Therefore, investors saw that there could be a long bureaucratic path between the executive order and the actual reclassification. The movement was quite conservative, probably so as not to disgrace the Republican Party.

Additionally, nothing has been defined regarding access to credit for companies in this sector, which could be a catalyst for shares. Finally, companies in the sector have relatively low trading volumes; therefore, sales movements by large investment funds have brought a lot of volatility to prices.

Why do I remain optimistic?

Although the executive order was less comprehensive than investors expected, it is part of a movement that began in 2023 under the Biden Administration; therefore, the reclassification process is moving slowly.

Therefore, it seems to me that the question is "when" it will be reclassified, not "if" it will be reclassified, especially with the announced Medicare pilot program, which gives the initiative a lot of legitimacy. In this sense, when the reclassification actually occurs, access to credit and adjustments to the cost structure of these companies will be a consequence.

The final step would be the possibility of these companies' shares leaving the over-the-counter market and being listed on the NYSE and NASDAQ. In this scenario, investors who hold these shares will be rewarded.

Finally, while investors are obsessed with news coming from the Trump administration, the main ETF companies reported excellent results in the last quarter and beat market expectations.

Valuation

Currently, MSOS companies trade at 1.7x sales, and in times of greater expectations for the industry, the ETF has traded above 3x sales. It is worth noting that other older ETFs were trading at 5x sales when the Biden administration began discussions about reclassification.

The historical average, already weighted by time, is 2x sales. If MSOS returns to these levels, the target would be $4.45 (17.6% upside). Therefore, I reiterate the recommendation to Buy, and the trigger for this repricing of multiples would be a combination of results and positive news.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As I said in my last article, there are several risks. It is clear that investors are obsessed with news regarding the reclassification, and as there is no deadline for responding, this wait could literally "tire" investors of a thesis that has been slowly evolving over the last few years. This would trigger more selling and a further decline in the share price.

There are several specific regulatory risks that permeate this case, such as the actions of the DEA and the FDA, not to mention that the executive order can simply be challenged in court and remain on hold for years.

In the context of holding companies, the three main companies represent almost 60% of the total composition of the ETF; therefore, specific problems in the business of these companies may not accurately portray the performance of the cannabis industry. Finally, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a Strong Sell on this ETF, which goes against my buy recommendation.

The Bottom Line

In this article, I intended to revisit the investment thesis in cannabis ETFs, especially MSOS. I talked about the reasons why ETFs and shares in the sector plummeted amid the Trump administration's executive order, and I was able to comment a little about the prospects.

In my opinion, although the process may take time, the question is when the reclassification with real impacts on the sector will occur, and not "if" it will occur. If this thesis comes to fruition, the investor will have to deal with patience and resilience, but this "anxiety" is reduced as companies publish promising results.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to Buy MSOS. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio remains attractive, and this asset can fulfill an interesting role as a bold position in a diversified portfolio of shares.

data.title: Dow Jones Tumbles 600 Points On Strait Of Hormuz Tensions And Private Credit Jitters, Support At 46,660 Is Key
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Major US stock indexes slid 1% on Thursday as a volatile mix of geopolitical instability and domestic credit concerns shook investor confidence.

The primary catalyst was a sharp spike in crude oil prices, which surged toward the $100-per-barrel threshold following reports of two tankers set ablaze in Iraqi waters. These incidents, attributed to apparent Iranian strikes, are part of a broader wave of attacks targeting energy and transport infrastructure across the Middle East.

The escalation was further underscored by a defiant stance from Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who suggested that the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global energy artery, should remain closed as a strategic lever of pressure.

This supply-side threat has effectively reignited fears of persistent inflation, hitting the financial sector particularly hard as traders brace for a potentially more aggressive economic environment.

Comments late last night by Iranian military officials about potential attacks on companies in the Gulf which support the US military may also be weighing on markets in early trade.

Compounding these worries, Wall Street is also closely monitoring mounting "jitters" within the private credit market, adding another layer of risk to an already fragile trading session.

S&P 500 Heatmap

Private credit fears on the rise

The $2 trillion private credit market is facing intense scrutiny as a series of recent defaults has sparked widespread concern among investors. This anxiety was amplified by a warning from the Swiss private equity firm Partners Group (PGPHF), which projected that default rates in the sector could potentially double over the next few years.

These mounting jitters triggered a significant sell-off in the financial sector, with the S&P 500 (SPX) financials index dropping 1.5%.

The impact was felt acutely among major institutional players, particularly after Morgan Stanley (MS) saw its shares tumble 4.3% following a decision to limit redemptions at one of its private credit funds.

This move follows similar restrictive actions taken by Blackstone (BX) and BlackRock (BLK) earlier this month, both of which saw their stock prices decline by more than 1%. Further rattling the market, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) moved to reduce the valuations of certain loans tied to private credit funds, contributing to broader losses for banking giants like Citigroup (C) and Goldman Sachs (GS), which both fell by more than 3%.

Oil reserves release only a temporary fix

The International Energy Agency said that the world is facing the biggest oil supply disruption ever. This comes as the IEA announced a coordinated emergency release of up to 400m barrels.

It’s a record amount, eclipsing the 182m barrel emergency release from 2022. We're still waiting for the IEA to provide full details of the release.

There are still concerns regarding the IEA-coordinated release, especially about the speed at which this oil will reach the market and whether it will be enough to tie up the market until we see oil flowing through the Strait of Hormuz again.

US Energy Secretary Wright, speaking late on Wednesday, said that the US will release 172 million barrels of oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. It will take about 120 days to deliver the fuel.

According to ING research, this works out to a US release of around 1.4m b/d. If you assume a similar timeline for other countries, that works out to 3.3m b/d- far short of the supply losses we are seeing from the Persian Gulf.

Either way, these releases are not a long-term fix, and the longer the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the probability of higher oil prices and supply shortfalls increases.

Wall Street outlook moving forward

Risk assets are likely to remain under pressure the longer the conflict drags on. The economic outlook has shifted, with concerns growing around the supply of various products, from fertilizers to helium, etc.

This is reflected by Goldman Sachs cutting their US economic outlook and raising their oil forecast for the second time in little over a week.

According to standardized economic models, a sustained 10% increase in oil prices typically adds 0.2 percentage points to the headline inflation rate and 0.04 points to core inflation. This same 10% surge generally drags down GDP growth by a tenth of a percentage point, though the severity of this slowdown can be mitigated by the productivity of domestic energy producers.

Beyond energy costs, the economy is also grappling with the restrictive weight of tighter financial conditions. Research indicates that for every 1 percentage point of tightening in the financial conditions index, GDP growth is reduced by a full point over the subsequent year. Currently, Goldman Sachs' financial conditions index has already tightened by 0.2 percentage points, signaling a measurable cooling effect on the broader economy.

Technical Analysis - Dow Jones

From a technical perspective, the Dow Jones recovered admirably after the weekend gap down, which saw the index open some 500 points lower than Friday's close.

The entire gap was closed on Monday, followed by another day of gains on Tuesday, but risk sentiment has since waned as markets begin to expect a drawn-out conflict in the Middle East.

This led to yesterday's significant selloff, which saw the Dow shed around 1000 points and print a massive bearish engulfing candle.

The price has fallen below its 20, 50, and 100-day Simple Moving Averages (SMAs). Notably, the 20-day SMA (light blue) and 50-day SMA (purple) are beginning to slope downward, signaling that the short-to-medium-term trend is firmly bearish.

However, the index is holding above a key support area for now at the 46,660 handle, with a daily close above this handle opening up a possible short-term bounce.

At this stage, though, the technicals may be at the mercy of the fundamental factors driving markets and need to be put in perspective.

Any significant developments on the geopolitical front could lead to a drastic change in the technical outlook as well.

Dow Jones Daily Chart, March 12, 2026

data.title: S&P 500 Was Due For A Tougher Year In '26
data.description:
This blog post was written in late December ’25 just to think through the various variables impacting equity market returns, but one of the easier ways to analyze historical market returns is to just assume that double-digit returns will not extend forever.

After three years of actual returns for the S&P 500 of:

  • ’25: +17.88%
  • ’24: +24.87%
  • ’23: +26.37%

it would be safe to assume that just a “reversion to the mean” type year for the S&P 500 would be single digits, either mildly negative or mildly positive.

What was interesting about 2025 is the strong returns of formerly dormant asset classes like international equity (and bonds too) and emerging market equity.

These so-called non-correlated trades went nowhere for years - international equity had its best year since 2006 - in 2025.

Now, Iran has changed the 2026 investment landscape entirely, and has tossed a wrench into the “rotational” trade that made sense, at least until the air strikes.

The difficulty is trying to distinguish stocks, sectors, and asset classes, which might suffer real impairment from Iran as the US works through this conflict, from the stocks, sectors, and asset classes that are simply correcting normally, albeit driven by the headlines.

The “Liberation Day” correction that happened from late January ’25 through early April ’25 saw a 20% peak-to-trough correction.

That’s the last period where investors saw a good dose of fear in terms of investor sentiment.

Looking at historical returns for the S&P 500, here’s the last period the S&P 500 saw similar returns to ’23 – ’25:

  • ’21: +28.75%
  • ’20: +18.2%
  • ’19: +31.8%

Except for 2019, these S&P 500 annual returns were monetary policy and “zero interest rate” driven, but guess what the S&P 500 returned in 2022?

’22: -18.11%

Quick summary: Someone once referred to the S&P 500 patterns of return as “sequencing of returns” and it’s a valid point, but the larger point is that after 2 -3 years of robust equity market returns, investors tend to see single-digit numbers.

That’s not a prediction, but patterns matter.

The US equity market probably needs a good dose of extreme fear to put in a tradeable bottom after the spike in crude oil.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Proof Of Commercialization With Rare Optionality
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum's (QBTS) stock performance has been very polarizing, with a 211% return in 2025 before falling 28% YTD. However, we feel that D-Wave's gains in commercial traction are uniquely differentiated from other quantum computing peers, as its recently acquired Quantum Circuits has engendered a dual-platform positioning that few other quantum pure-plays can match.

That said, the quantum computing group as a whole is highly speculative, and D-Wave is a high-risk, high-conviction long, while trading at high valuations bereft of profits and with dilution risks.

A revenue base that starts small but is real

D-Wave reported FY2025 revenue of $24.6M, up 179% from FY2024's $8.8M. By segment, system sales achieved $16.2M, Quantum Computing as a Service contributed $5.5M, while $2.7M came from professional services. GAAP gross margins expanded 19.6 percentage points Y/Y to 82.6%.

We note that revenue in FY25 was front-loaded, as the first quarter was $15M, driven by the Jülich system sale. If we back out the remaining nine months, it would be $9.6M or $3.2M per quarter. This works out to be ~45% Y/Y if we exclude the one-off, lumpy revenue spike in 1Q25. In 4Q25 alone, revenue was $2.8M, having increased 19.2% Y/Y. While we would not penalize a nascent company like QBTS for having lumpy revenue, we advise caution in simply reading headline, annualized figures.

In addition, the company does not engage in circular financing, as stated in its opening remarks on the earnings call:

We do not give, grant, invest or lend funds to any of our customers that they utilize or intend to utilize towards the purchase of our products and/or services.

CFO John Markovich

This should help put to rest some of the concerns over quantum stocks' revenue quality. It served over 135 individual customers in FY25, with over 70 commercial customers. Needless to say, we believe that their customer base is well diversified for a relatively small company.

Inflection is in the bookings

While we are encouraged by the revenue story in FY25, we detect a more valuable signal in its bookings data. An eight-figure system sale in FY24 boosted total annual bookings during that year to $23.9M, making its FY25 bookings, which totaled $18.7M, look quite soft. However, bookings in 4Q25 were $13.4M, which was the second-highest quarter for the company. Further, QBTS closed an additional $32.8M in bookings in 1Q26. This included a $20M sale to Florida Atlantic University and a two-year, $10M enterprise QCaaS agreement with a Fortune 100 firm.

As noted by the CFO on the earnings call, prospective transactions are likely to experience a seven-fold surge along with a potential 1,500% Y/Y growth in its pipeline in FY26E.

Market consensus puts FY26E revenue at $43.6M, which reflects a 77% Y/Y growth figure. This looks very attainable to us, as more than half of that projection is likely closed under the 1Q26 sales/bookings.

Quantum Circuits acquisition to shift the calculus

In January 2026, D-Wave finished its acquisition of Quantum Circuits for ~$550M, comprised of $300M in stock and $250M in cash. This acquisition aims to solve a full range of its customers' complex computational problems with its annealing and gate-model quantum computing systems, whose build-out will be significantly accelerated by this combination.

The strategy employed here is straightforward. D-Wave has been the pioneer for annealing quantum computing for over two decades and is optimized for combinatorial problems, notably in applications for logistics, scheduling, financial modeling, and defense. On the other hand, gate-model quantum computing is the broader paradigm pursued by its peers, IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti (RGTI), IBM (IBM), and Google (GOOGL). The latter is used in drug discovery, materials science, and machine learning. This business combination increases the latitude of QBTS' addressable market.

CEO Alan Baratz described on the latest call the meaningfully differentiated technology Quantum Circuits brings. It uses a dual-rail qubit architecture, which achieves logical qubit ratios of roughly 1 logical qubit per 100-200 physical qubits, compared to approximately 1:1,000-2,000 in conventional transmon-based superconducting designs. Gate speeds are a thousand times faster without compromising on fidelity. Also, the company demonstrated on-chip cryogenic control of gate-model qubits in January this year, which the management touted as an industry first and a key enabler in scaling.

But of course, we should be mindful of execution risks. In its current laboratory stage, the company still needs to bring it into a commercially viable product alongside its established annealing business. We are hopeful that the company can build a prevailing moat around its dual-platform model.

Gaining traction among defense/government verticals

The company entered into a collaboration with Anduril Industries and Davidson Technologies in January 2026, which strengthens its position in the U.S. air and missile defense arena. D-Wave's Stride hybrid solver was notably faster and had a higher mitigation rate compared to classical-only methods. The CEO mentioned during the same earnings call that the Anduril demonstration had helped the product garner a very significant inflow of interest.

D-Wave is not simply going after research grants; it is aiming to address a sizable defense and federal optimization market with a dedicated business unit. This contrasts with other peers who target government subsidies for their research, which may never be commercially viable.

Making sense of the financials

Quite frankly, GAAP statements are not helping in analyzing the future profitability of companies like QBTS with early-stage products. We certainly appreciate that the company has already reached an initial commercialization stage with small but meaningful revenue. On profitability, we strip out non-cash/non-operating charges relating to its fair value changes in its warrant liabilities (gets worse if stock price goes up), and we get an adjusted net loss of $84.5M or -$0.26 per share in FY25. The net loss widened from FY24's $75.6M.

Working our way up the income statement, we have adjusted EBITDA at a loss of -$71.8M in FY25. On a GAAP basis, operating expenses were divided into $50.7M in R&D, $41.2M in G&A, and $28.8M in S&M, which totals $120.7M. Adjusted (non-GAAP) operating expenses were reduced to $92.9M, as $21.9M of stock-based compensation was taken out.

Consolidated operating expense is guided to grow ~15% per quarter throughout FY26. This is mainly due to the integration of Quantum Circuits, new hiring, and the Boca Raton R&D center development. Our math points to a quarterly run rate of near $50M by 4Q26E, from ~$35M starting in 1Q26E. Given market projections of $43.6M in revenue for FY26E, we believe adjusted EBITDA will worsen to a net loss of -$130M.

Balance sheet and dilution risks

D-Wave ended the year with ~$885M in cash and marketable securities. During FY25, the company raised over $800M through selling shares on the market, an ELOC facility, and warrant exercises. Total debt is minimal at $7.6M

Adjusting for the Quantum Circuits purchase, the company should have $635M cash in January. We compute a conservative cash burn rate of $25M per quarter based on the sequential operating expense increase guidance. The current cash pile can serve about 6 years of runway. On the same earnings call, CFO Markovich appears confident about bringing the company to profitability before cash runs out.

The company filed a $330M shelf, hinting further additional raises through equity are imminent, following a massive dilution in FY25, which saw shares outstanding grow 35% Y/Y even before the share raise for the Quantum Circuits deal.

Peer comparison

The whole quantum pure-play group trades absent of positive earnings, with only P/S as the only comparable metric. That said, P/S ratios are all too high to be meaningfully compared.

D-Wave emphasizes that it has a diverse set of customers, including commercial ones. But, it has a large customer covering 67% of total revenue in FY25, most likely from the Jülich system sale. IonQ doesn't disclose total customers, but the top 3 customers covered 61% of revenue in 9M25. Rigetti is essentially a government contractor, with over 90% of total revenue from the government in FY25.

Metric D-Wave IonQ Rigetti
FY2025 Revenue $24.6M $130M $7.1M
FY2025 Rev Growth +179% Y/Y +202% Y/Y -34% Y/Y
Market Cap (approx.) ~$6.9B ~$12.6B ~$5.6B
P/S (TTM) ~280x ~100x ~800x
Cash Position $884.5M $2,392M $444M
FY2026E Revenue $43.6M $225-245M (guided) $23M

Source: Companies, Himalayas Research estimates

Risks

Question of quantum supremacy. While research reported (also here) by Science confirmed D-Wave's core scientific claim with its Advantage2 system, it did not excel in all scenarios. A method using a competing classical approach produced results with less errors than the quantum annealer in certain cases involving 2D and 3D systems. However, there was significant doubt classical approaches could outperform in infinite dimension systems.

Timeline risk. There are skeptics that believe quantum error correction is near impossible to fix or may be decades away. If the quantum thesis takes longer to realize than the market judges, the entire industry will de-rate.

Dilution risk. As mentioned before, the company has recently filed a $330M shelf, and in line with its dilution history in the past, the stock will have a bigger hurdle to clear as outstanding shares keep increasing.

Verdict

In our view, D-Wave is a commercially advanced quantum pure-play with a growing, diverse set of customers riding on its unique dual-platform strategy. Revenue, despite being small, is genuine and is a strong proof of concept of commercialization. Its balance sheet is strong but comes at a dilution risk for its shareholders.

Near-term financials are not indicative of where this stock or industry trades, so P/S ratios are meaningless.

We think QBTS is a speculative BUY for investors who want optionality in quantum computing. In that case, QBTS offers an optimal bet as it is a company on solid footing, with a proven product and encouraging financials for an upstart. Positive catalysts to watch out for—1) conversion of its bookings into revenue for FY26E, 2) delivery and customer uptake of its initial gate-model system in late 2026, and 3) more government and defense contracts beyond Anduril.

data.title: CRAK: How The Conflict In Iran Is Playing In Favor Of Oil Refineries
data.description:
There is an ETF that is standing out in the energy landscape in positive terms, with a six-month return above +30%, directly linked to the energy shock following the opening of the conflict in Iran: we are talking about the VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK). Exactly… the increase in the crack spread and the presence of a favorable political and market context seem to be generating a distinctive revaluation for the prices of the companies that constitute this ETF, even compared to more generalized energy segments such as the one represented, for example, by the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). By conducting an analysis on three levels of drivers and on two possible scenarios, I tend to remain positive in a prospective sense for this ETF. But before getting into the heart of the issue…

What is CRAK?

It is a sectoral and thematic equity ETF focused on global oil refineries that uses the MVIS Global Oil Refiners Index as its benchmark with an expense ratio of 0.62%. A cost covered by the dividend yield of 1.60% (not distinctive but still positive).

The index strategy

This index is rules-based and modified capitalization weighted and tracks the performance of the shares of companies involved in crude oil refining and in the production of products such as gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, fuel oil, naphtha, and other petrochemicals. In fact, companies must generate at least 50% of their revenues from crude oil refining to be included. They must then have a market cap above $150 million, a 3-month average daily trading volume of at least $1 million, and a minimum monthly volume of 250,000 shares over the last six months. In the ETF, the weighting method used is modified market capitalization weighting, with a maximum weight per single stock equal to 8%

It should therefore be read as a tool to invest in refining margins and in crack spreads, not as a linear proxy for the price of oil.

The holding distribution

The fund today incorporates fewer than 30 holdings, which implies a fairly concentrated distribution toward a few solutions. Not surprisingly, the top 10 is about 60% of the portfolio: a non-negligible element. Of these, 88.35% are large cap, 11.52% mid cap, and essentially no small cap exposure.

Likewise, the sector focus that emerges is clearly extreme: in practice, 100% is in the Energy sector, to which a well-distributed global geographic diversification is added. In fact, CRAK is not dominated by the United States, and over 70% of the exposure is international. So I want to say that the fund incorporates not only an industrial cycle, but also geographic, geopolitical, and currency risk.

Peers

To understand the difference, in my opinion, it is essential to make a conceptual comparison, not between specific tickers but between CRAK and traditional energy/classic oil ETFs. This is because the key difference that emerges is that CRAK does not invest generically in oil & gas, integrated majors or upstream, but in refineries, and it follows that (at least in technical terms) its main driver is not the price of crude oil itself, but the crack spread. Hence, the divergence in performance compared to the broader and more commonly used XLE, also with a generalized energy theme.

And it is in this sense interesting, indeed introductory, to note how CRAK has maintained periods of clear outperformance, such as the one that began in 2025.

But also periods of clear underperformance, such as the one that began in 2020 and continued from 2022 until precisely the end of 2025.

Catalysts

The portfolio built in this way has been described as highly exposed to crack spreads, global fuel demand, bottlenecks in refining capacity, and regional differentials between feedstock and refined products. In other words, the portfolio should, in theory, perform well when the favorable differential between crude oil and refined products widens.

In fact, in theory, the temporary decrease in refining capacity pushes the prices of refined fuels (diesel, gasoline) relative to crude oil, increasing the profitability of refiners. Since refining reacts differently compared to oil upstream, the performance of the refining segment could outperform the broader energy sector in this phase.

The other two drivers

To understand this, it is necessary to read the historical periods that we have gone through from 2016 to today (the last decade). This reading alone is enough to understand that what drives CRAK’s return is not simply the crack spread alone. This table should summarize it well.

It seems clear to me that the drivers to consider, beyond the crack spread, are the structure of the energy cycle and the pricing itself of the equity markets. So let’s try to conduct a broader context analysis:

  1. Crack spreads are widening, the profitability of refineries increases, the first point in favor of CRAK. The shock in Iran is hitting refined products, so there is a geopolitical catalyst to consider.

  2. We are in a phase of a positive energy cycle, like the one of the Trump cycle 1 of 2016-2020, in which a favorable energy context for the oil sector is present.

  3. We are in a context of sector rotation toward defensive segments, among which energy plays in favor of the expansion of the multiples of ETFs like CRAK, which in recent years have instead lagged behind the tech sector.

A scenario analysis

If, therefore, there are hypothetically two possible scenarios, which of these is more likely?

Simply put, since all three growth drivers of CRAK are positive, in my opinion, it is the structural scenario. And trying to contextualize, I expect in the short term a continuation of the current positive reaction, in parallel with the duration of the conflict, for the CRAK ETF; in the medium term, the reaction is more uncertain on the crack spread, but there still remains a favorable energy context, and an expansionary pricing of the equity markets.

Risk

Naturally, the energy shock of the conflict may be short-lived, or in any case, the increase in the crack spread may not justify an increase in the profitability of refiners. It certainly would not be the first time this happened, and 2022 is an example. After the energy shock caused by the Russian sanctions inspired by the US, Russian diesel blocked by the EU embargo was replaced by supplies from the US, the Middle East, India, China, and Korea. In other words, there was no real shock in supply, even though the crack spread had increased. This type of reorganization (re-export and reallocation) contained the impact on refining margins, leaving room for other energy companies to benefit from the increase in energy prices to the detriment of the average annual returns of CRAK.

In this sense, the shock on margins would be transitory, and the market would correct its aim on the valuations of CRAK stocks, while the general increase in energy inflation would widen the revenues of traditional energy companies like those in XLE.

This confirms the fact that statistically, crack spreads historically return to their average (typically $10-$20) and distillate inventories rebuild quickly. Therefore, the tendency to have extra profits on the part of the companies in CRAK is not a structural element, but rather a transitory one. Despite this, the cycles of widening and narrowing have allowed CRAK to maintain a linearity in returns compared to XLE, which significantly reduces the specific risk, accentuating, in my opinion, the expected risk-return ratio, pushed higher by the three positive drivers.

Conclusion

Today, however, the situation clearly appears different: the energy shock does not come from sanctions but from the conflict itself. It will therefore be the duration of the conflict that determines the extent of the widening in the profitability of the companies within the CRAK ETF. But despite this variable, there are also three other drivers not to forget: a stimulating energy context, like the one of 2016–2020 of the first Trump administration, and a process of sector rotation toward defensive segments, such as energy itself. In this sense, I therefore feel aligned with the SA Quant rating in giving a Buy rating to the CRAK ETF.

data.title: Silver Rejects Triangle Formation From Iran War - Breakout Scenarios
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Ongoing war flows have been very different from what the trading and investing world has been accustomed to.

Safe-haven assets like metals (particularly gold and silver) and bonds have not seen any consistent demand. At the same time, stock markets and risk sentiment in general have remained very solid despite stagflationary fears.

Only oil and, consequently, the petrodollar, have managed to grab consistent attention and generate persistent trends.

One question remains for metals aficionados – where is the flight to demand towards them?

Their previous run higher throughout their borderline insane performance from mid-2025 to January 2026 could explain exhausted buyers.

When people get heavily positioned in an asset class, buyers run out of bullets (to buy more), supply increases to capture the higher values, and it distorts the previous stock shortage that led to previous price increases. This heavy positioning was outlined in the recent Bank of America survey.

As a matter of fact, the ongoing war could be what is holding silver and metals prices at current levels – after the late January price tumble, their correction suddenly stalled at a similar time that WTI began to rally and the ongoing US-Iran war began to enter market discussions.

So that could open the door for a large correction in the event of a war resolution!

Still, the global economy is largely different than it was just a few years ago, with the second round of Trump Trade Wars, de-globalization and persistent rise of new conflicts.

It wouldn't be surprising to never see $20 silver prices again, but $50 is not completely out of the picture. In the meantime, traders can profit from the hesitant price action by playing ongoing consolidation around the asset classes.

We will dive into a silver multi-timeframe analysis to spot the current range in the metal and explore potential breakout levels. Let's get right into it.

Silver (XAG/USD) Multi-timeframe Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

Silver has now remained in an $80 to $96 range since the end of February, holding the top of its 2025 (broken) upward channel as support – a major technical indicator to track bull strength.

RSI momentum has died down in the higher timeframe, indicating current equilibrium around current prices. This confirms after the failed breakdown in early March and consequent failed breakout.

This provides quality indications for a range-bound price action.

Let's take a closer look

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Looking closer, bears are entering at the highs of the triangle formation, pointing to interesting tests coming ahead:

Breaking the $84.50 support trendline (from the 2025 channel) would hint at the $80 support

  • Holding the support would confirm the range towards a $90 rebound
  • Breaking it, however, could lead to retesting the $70 2026 support

Rebounding on the support trendline could lead to an imminent rebound towards the $90 to $92 resistance (less probable with current RSI conditions)

Levels to watch for Silver (XAG) trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $87 session highs (mini-resistance)
  • Key Range Resistance $90 to $92
  • Higher Timeframe Major Resistance $90 to $95
  • $96.47 March highs

Support Levels:

  • Triangle Support $84.50 (short-term bearish below)
  • 2025 Record Pivot $80 to $82
  • Key Momentum Support $76 to $77.50
  • Major 2026 Support $70 to $72
  • December FOMC Minor Support $60 to $64 (Feb Lows)
  • $50 to $54 Major Support

1H Chart

With the hesitation also spreading to the shorter timeframes, traders could await for either:

A break below the mini-support $84.50 to play a breakdown to $80

  • Ongoing selling should take the action there soon!

A break above the daily $87.50 to play a breakout to $92

Original Post

data.title: Cisco Q2: The Valuation Makes No Sense
data.description:
On Feb 11, 2026, Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) reported second-quarter FY26 earnings that, on paper, looked like a clean win. Revenue came in at $15.35 billion, beating estimates by 1.49%. Diluted EPS (non-GAAP), meanwhile, landed at $1.04 a share, topping estimates by 1.75%.

More importantly, both numbers cleared the higher end of Cisco’s own guidance, and this quarter marked a record revenue for the company.

But the stock dropped about 9% after the results went out. At the same time, Cisco is positioning itself at the center of AI infrastructure buildouts.

That, and with the other developments outlined in this analysis, my Buy rating for Cisco remains in place.

Cisco Stock Profile & Valuation

Before getting into the financials, it would make sense to see where the stock stands today and whether the price tag is justified.

At the time of this publication, Cisco trades at around $78 per share. Year-to-date, the stock is up about 1.4% or so, but zooming out to a 1-year scale shows a return of roughly 29%, and a >60% return over the past 5 years.

But of course, growth isn’t linear, at least when it comes to stocks, and we can see it in the days following earnings. Though the broader trend is still up and is moving in the right, or let me say, in a profitable direction.

In terms of valuation metrics, Cisco currently trades at a forward P/E (GAAP) of about 25.43x, which is approximately 13% below the sector median.

This means investors are paying $25.43 for every $1 of Cisco’s expected earnings, and, by traditional measures, the company is trading at a discount relative to its peers.

To me, this is noteworthy considering that this company generates over $60 billion in annual revenue and sits at the inner circle of AI infrastructure development. Yet, for some reason, the company is trading at a discount to the average tech logo. That, to me, screams undervaluation, which generally doesn’t last long.

Now, Cisco’s PEG ratio (TTM) sits at 1.26x, and this is about 41% above the sector median. That might look overly expensive at first glance, but the trailing earnings growth rate is being measured against a sector full of mature tech companies growing at low single digits. Cisco, on the other hand, delivered 31% EPS growth.

Moreover, most of the AI revenue is yet to be recognized, since Cisco’s big ramp starts in FY26. So, the least common denominator in that PEG calculation is capturing a transition period and not the real run-rate growth this business is heading to. Once AI revenue starts coming in, I see the PEG compressing pretty quickly.

With that in mind, let’s get into the business stands today.

What Was In Cisco's Second Quarter Fiscal 2026 Report?

In the most recent quarter, revenue rose 10% to $15.3 billion, with the heavy lifting coming from the product side, which rose 14% year over year to $11 billion. And of course, the star of the show was networking, growing at 21% to $8.3 billion. This is the segment that sells AI networking equipment, so a 21% increase potentially tells us that customers are spending heavily on the pipelines that carry the data supporting AI workloads.

On the profitability side, operating income rose 21% to $3.8 billion, with operating margins expanding 2.3 percentage points to 24.6%. Operating expenses, on the other hand, rose a bit by 3% to $6.2 billion. This encompasses everything from R&D spending to admin costs. As a result, net income rose 31% to $3.2 billion, or $0.80 a share (GAAP).

One thing worth flagging, though. Cisco’s effective tax rate dropped to 12.9% (from 15.9%), which partially boosted its bottom-line figures for the quarter. That lower tax rate may not necessarily be something we can count on repeating every quarter, so it’s worth keeping it in mind when projecting forward earnings.

So, if Cisco beat estimates and posted record revenue, what happened to the stock?

Why Did Cisco's Stock Drop After Earnings?

I think the selloff potentially boils down to two things: next quarter’s GAAP EPS guidance and non-GAAP gross margins guidance.

Specifically, GAAP EPS guidance for the third quarter of FY26 is between $0.73 and $0.77, which would become a sequential decline from the $0.80 Cisco just reported. Management is saying that Cisco will be less profitable in the next quarter compared to the recent one. Of course, nobody wants to hear that, especially after reporting a banner quarter. And no, the market didn’t like it.

Another one is its non-GAAP gross margin guidance, and I think investors are also concerned about it. The next quarter’s guided non-GAAP gross margin is set to come in at 65.5% and 66.5%, which is also a sequential step down from the current 67.5%. Well, guided margins aside, the reported GAAP and non-GAAP margins also experienced notable setbacks, mainly due to rising memory chip costs. As CFO Mark Patterson stated during the earnings call:

Total non-GAAP gross margin came in at 67.5%, down 120 basis points year-over-year. Non-GAAP product gross margin was 66.4%, down 130 basis points, primarily driven by negative impacts from mix and higher memory costs, partially offset by productivity improvements.

It makes sense. Memory chips are key components in networking hardware, and when prices go up, Cisco’s cost of goods moves in lockstep. Management noted that advanced purchase commitments increased by $1.8 billion in the last 90 days alone, mainly to lock in memory supply, so they’re quite upfront and transparent about it.

On that subject, the increase in cost is completely out of Cisco’s control, which is why I think the post-earnings selloff was an overreaction. You wouldn’t cut a $1.8 billion check to secure components if you expected costs to ease anytime soon, would you?

That covers the near-term pain. On the other side of the equation, there’s a significant growth catalyst that, I believe, is underappreciated and is why I think Cisco’s valuation is disjointed.

What Could Push The Stock Higher?

The reason to own Cisco right now is the sheer size of the AI infrastructure opportunity in its order book. Specifically, AI orders from hyperscale and cloud customers for the quarter increased by more than 61% to $2.1 billion compared to the same period last year.

That’s an impressive pace, and as a result, management raised annual AI order expectations to over $5 billion. Should guidance materialize as expected, it would imply a nearly 140% year-over-year increase in annual AI-related orders.

Apart from hyperscale customers, Cisco was able to diversify AI order backlogs to smaller entities. According to CEO Charles Robbins:

Beyond hyperscalers, we have a separate AI opportunity across neocloud, sovereign and enterprise customers. We took $350 million in AI orders from these customers in Q2 and have a growing pipeline in excess of $2.5 billion for our high-performance AI infrastructure portfolio. We continue to develop our strategic partnerships to capture this opportunity.

I think this diversification is a critical development that isn’t fully priced in. Now, a backlog is only worth something if there’s real evidence it’ll convert into actual revenue. During the quarter, Cisco shipped its 1-millionth Silicon One chip and introduced the 102.4 terabits-per-second G300 chip, which is, by far, the most powerful networking chip Cisco has ever made.

Cisco also launched the P200 product line, which is another high-performance networking chip. Now, it’s worth noting that neither the G300 nor the P200 is included in the $5 billion AI order target yet, so the headline number everyone is anchoring doesn’t account for demand from Cisco’s newest products. This is an underappreciated upside catalyst sitting outside the official forecasts.

On the contracts side, Cisco’s total remaining performance obligations, or RPOs, have increased 5% to $43.4 billion year-over-year. This is the total dollar value of contracts Cisco has already signed but hasn’t yet recognized as revenue.

Within the total RPO, there’s the long-term product RPO, which increased 11% year-over-year to $11.8 billion. So, Cisco has $43.4 billion in committed future revenue, with a product-specific portion that’s growing faster than the total. That’s a good sign of product demand.

Now, management expects to recognize over $3 billion in AI revenue from hyperscalers alone in FY’26. If Cisco delivers on that guidance and order momentum holds, the AI infrastructure segment alone could make up 5 to 6% of the company’s total revenue. It’s a small slice of the pie, indeed, but considering Cisco’s AI revenue was basically nonexistent about 2 years ago, that’s a notable change in revenue mix.

And considering that the G300 and P200s are still in the early innings, I believe AI orders could exceed the guided $5 billion in FY’27. Personally, I think the payoff also shows up on the earnings line. For example, Cisco’s OpEx increased only 3% this quarter, so the cost is barely moving. When the backlog starts converting to recognized revenue at current product margins (63 to 64%), a much larger portion of the revenue will flow straight to the bottom lines.

What's The Risk Around Cisco?

Now, with every investment opportunity, it’s critical to take a balanced view of what could go wrong. The core issue Cisco faces is rising memory costs. According to reports, High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) prices have surged ~400% year-over-year and could rise even higher. Well, management has acknowledged that these increases hit with virtually zero lead time. In fact, this is the primary driver behind the 120 basis point decline in non-GAAP gross margin in the recent quarter and the expected sequential decline in the next quarter.

Cisco is actively working to offset this. In fact, it was managed using three levers: price increases for customers, revised contract terms with partners, and taking advantage of Cisco’s purchasing power in supplier negotiations, which resulted in the 73% year-over-year increase in inventory. To me, these are sensible moves, and I expect them to gradually take hold.

The reality, though, is that Patterson also acknowledged these measures take time to flow through. He said during the earnings call:

And then in terms of the timing, I think the measures that we talked about in terms of price increases and then some of the Ts and Cs that we're going to be modifying with our partners and customers, those just take a little bit of time to run through, so you'll start to see that over time.

The thing is, Cisco’s fastest-growing segment is the AI infrastructure, which also happens to be the most memory-intensive. So, as AI orders ramp up, memory exposure ramps up with them. No, I don’t think this dynamic will last forever, as supply commitments will eventually catch up. But, in the near term, it does create a situation where revenue growth can coexist with margin pressure.

What's My Verdict For Cisco?

The debate right now is about the next 2 quarters. Will margins finally stabilize? Will the cost of memory ease? Both are fair arguments, but they are short-term. I think the bigger picture is that Cisco is positioning itself to be one of those that sell the entire foundation layer of how AI gets connected globally.

Apart from what the numbers already show, what gives me confidence is the simple observation about how AI spending is maturing. Right now, the market rewards those who are selling picks and shovels- in this case, it’s the GPUs and chips, but the bottleneck is gradually moving downstream.

The networking layer potentially becomes the next bottleneck, and that’s where Cisco comes into play. On that note, I think the market is still pricing the company as a secondary beneficiary of AI spending, even when industry trends suggest it’s becoming a primary one.

With that and everything else outlined above, my Buy rating for Cisco stays in place.
